content.full_text:
Farn Por-rrrcar- PRACTTcEs CoMMrssroN
P.O. Box 807 o 428 J Street o Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(9t6) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
Iune 25,2002
Kellie Bewley
Amalgamated Transit Union
Local 1555
132 Ninth Street, Suite 100
Oakland, CA94607-4712
Re
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-02-126
Dear Ms. Bewley:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Amalgamated
TransitUnion regarding the Proposition 34 provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").1
QUESTION
What are the contribution limits applicable to your union regarding Assembly,
Senate, state, and local elections?
CONCLUSION
The contribution limits for the offices mentioned are stated below
FACTS
You request direction as to your Union Local 1555 and the spending limitations
for Assembly, Senate, state, and local elections. The Act does not impose a mandatory
expenditure or "spending" limit, unless voluntarily undertaken (Section 85400 of the
Act). However, when these expenditure limits do apply, they only apply to candidate
expenditures. We therefore have understood your question to concern the Act's
contribution limits.
I Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regrlations.


File No. A-02-126
Page No. 2
ANALYSN
Section 85301 of the Act outlines limitations2 from persons to candidates as
follows
"(a) A person. . .may not make to any candidate for elective state offrce[']. . .uny
contribution totaling more than three thousand dollars ($3,000) per election."
"(b) Except to a candidate for Governor, a person. . .may not make to any
candidate for statewide elective office[a]. . . any contribution totaling more than
five thousand dollars ($5,000) per election."
"(c) A person...may not make to any candidate for Governor...any contribution
totaling more than twenty thousand dollars ($20,000) per election."
"Person" as defined in Section 82047 of the Act, is "an individual, proprietorship,
firm, partnership, joint venture, syndicate, business trust, company, corporation, limited
liability company, association, committee, and any other organization or group of persons
acting in concert." Your union would qualify as a "person."
Thus, a person may generally contribute six thousand ($6,000) to an assembly
candidate- three thousand ($3,000) for the primary election and three thousand ($3,000)
for the general election.
Section 85302 of the Political Reform Act outlines limitations from small
contributor committees to candidates as follows:
"(a) A small contributor committee may not make to any candidate for elective
state office...any contribution totaling more than six thousand dollars ($6,000)
per election.
"(b) Except to a candidate for Governor, a small contributor committee may not
make to any candidate for statewide elective offrce... any contribution totaling
more than ten thousand dollars ($10,000) per election.
"(c) A small contributor committee may not make to any candidate for
Governor.. . any contribution totaling more than twenty thousand dollars (20,000)
per election."
2 "Election" for the purposes of the Ac! "mears any primary, general, special or recall election held in
this state. The primary and general or special elections are separate elections . . . ." for purposes of the limits.
($ 82022.)
3 "Elective State Office' is defined as the office of Governor, Lieutenant Governor, Attomey General,
Insurance Commissioner, Controller, Secretary of State, Treasurer, Superintendent of Public Instruction,
Member of the Legislature, members elected to the Board of Administration of the Public Employees'
Retirement System, and member of the State Board of Equalization." ($ 82024.)
" "Statelvide Elective Office" is defined as the above-stated offices withthe exception of Members of the
Legislature, members elected to the Board of Adrninistration of the Public Employees' Retirement System.
($ 82053, emphasis added.)


File No. A-02-126
Page No. 3
The Act's contribution limits do not apply to elections for local office.
A small contributor committee, in Section 85203 of the Act, means any
committee that meets all of the following criteria:
"(a) The committee has been in existence for at least six months.
(b) The committee receives contributions from 100 or more persons.
(c) No one person has contributed to the committee more than two hundred
dollars ($200) per calendar year.
(d) The committee makes contributions to five or more candidates." (Also see
regulation 18503)
"Committee," as defined in Section 82013, means any person or combination of
persons who directly or indirectly does any of the following:
"(a) Receives contributions totaling one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more in a
calendar year.
(b) Makes independent expenditures totaling one thousand dollars ($1,000) or
more in a calendar year; or
(c) Makes contributions totaling ten thousand dollars ($10,000) or more in a
calendar year to or at the behest of candidates or committees."
We do not know if you qualify as a small contributor committee, but we have
provided this as general advice for your information. You should seek further written
advice if you have a specific question about the Act's campaign reporting and other
requirements. We have enclosed a copy of campaign manuals and an addendum to the
manual to further assist you.
Much of this information is also available on our website and I have enclosed
printouts from the website for your assistance. If you have any other questions regarding
this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
<_
By: Anthony Pane
Intern, Legal Division
Enclosures

sections.question:
What are the contribution limits applicable to your union regarding Assembly,
Senate, state, and local elections?

expenditures. We therefore have understood your question to concern the Act's
contribution limits.
